does: This paper demonstrates that adversaries can successfully execute harmful tasks by strategically combining multiple AI models, even when each model individually passes safety evaluations. The authors study two decomposition approaches: manual decomposition where humans break tasks into subtasks suited to different models, and automated decomposition where a weaker model generates benign intermediate tasks that a stronger aligned model solves, whose outputs are then leveraged to accomplish malicious goals. Through empirical evaluation, they show this strategy enables generation of vulnerable code, explicit images, hacking scripts, and manipulative content—capabilities that individual models alone would refuse.
connects: This work builds on and extends prior AI safety research on model evaluation and misuse detection. It responds to the standard assumption underlying most safety evaluations that testing individual models in isolation is sufficient to prevent harm. The paper connects to broader literature on adversarial attacks against language models, capability evaluation methodologies, and the emerging understanding that system-level interactions can create emergent risks. It also relates to work on task decomposition and in-context learning, drawing on observations that models can leverage chain-of-thought and multi-step reasoning to accomplish goals they would otherwise refuse.
enables: This research highlights critical gaps in current safety evaluation practices and points toward several future directions. It motivates the development of more holistic safety testing that considers not just individual model capabilities but also interactions between models in realistic deployment scenarios. The work enables future research on defensive countermeasures, such as detecting when model outputs are being chained together for harmful purposes, designing training procedures that prevent subtask-based circumvention, and developing evaluation frameworks that test model combinations. It also opens questions about governance approaches needed when diverse AI systems are deployed simultaneously in accessible ecosystems.
